terms.definition: The person who starts the court case by filing the petition.
The person who responds to the petition filed by the petitioner.
The legal process of ending a marriage or domestic partnership.
A court order that allows spouses to live apart while remaining legally married.
Property acquired during the marriage, generally divided equally.
Property owned before marriage or acquired by gift/inheritance during marriage.
The legal authority to make decisions about a child's welfare and where they live.
The right to make important decisions about a child's health, education, and welfare.
Determines which parent the child lives with on a day-to-day basis.
The right of the non-custodial parent to spend time with the child.
Court-ordered payments from one parent to another for the financial support of their children.
Court-ordered payments from one spouse to the other after separation or divorce.
A court order that protects a person from being harassed, threatened, or stalked.
Abuse or threats of abuse between people in close relationships.
A written statement of facts signed under penalty of perjury.
Document proving that legal papers were properly delivered to the other party.
Court approval to waive filing fees for people who cannot afford them.
When a respondent fails to file a response within the required time.
The final court order that resolves the case.
A notice served on the respondent informing them a case has been filed.
Submitting legal documents to the court clerk for official processing.
A court proceeding where a judge hears arguments and evidence.
A formal request to the court to take a specific action.
A directive issued by a judge that parties must follow.
A process where a neutral third party helps resolve disputes.
An agreement between parties on an issue, submitted to the court.
The legal requirement to share financial information with the other party.
A court proceeding or order made at the request of one party without notice to the other.
Postponement of a court hearing to a later date.
The legal act of delivering court documents to the opposing party.
The legal standard courts use when making custody and visitation decisions.
An arrangement where both parents share decision-making and/or time with the child.
An arrangement where one parent has primary authority over the child.
A document detailing how parents will share responsibilities and time with their children.
A court ruling that a marriage is legally invalid.
A legal relationship providing similar rights to marriage for qualifying couples.
A written agreement between spouses resolving property, support, and custody issues.
Willful disobedience of a court order.
A change to an existing court order based on changed circumstances.
Uniform Child Custody Jurisdiction and Enforcement Act — determines which state has authority over custody.
The legal authority of a court to hear and decide a case.
The specific court location where a case is heard.
The formal process of exchanging information and evidence between parties.
Sworn testimony given outside of court, recorded by a court reporter.
A court order requiring a person to appear in court or produce documents.
The crime of lying under oath in a court proceeding or on a legal document.
A person who represents themselves in court without an attorney.
The lawyer officially designated to represent a party in a case.
A court-appointed person who represents a child's best interests in legal proceedings.
An attorney appointed by the court to represent the interests of a child.
A request to relocate with a child to a different geographic area.
A court order directing an employer to deduct support payments from wages.
Unpaid amounts of support that are past due.
A document transferring property interest from one person to another without guarantees.
A court order dividing retirement benefits between spouses (QDRO).
Separating the divorce into two parts — ending the marriage while other issues are still pending.
A judgment that ends the marriage while reserving other issues for later resolution.
The date one spouse expressed intent to end the marriage, affecting property division.
Standard orders in family law summons preventing both parties from certain actions.
Court-connected services providing custody mediation and recommendations.
